questions.text: What is the most common indicator used by the World Bank to compare the development of different countries?
questions.explanation: The World Bank's World Development Reports use per capita income (average income) as the primary criterion for classifying countries.

questions.text: Developmental goals of different sections of our society can be achieved by:
questions.explanation: In a democracy, different groups can negotiate and achieve their goals through peaceful political participation.

questions.text: If women are engaged in paid work, their ________ in the household and society increases.
questions.explanation: Paid work provides financial independence, which enhances a woman's status and respect in society.

questions.text: Which of the following is a non-material goal of development?
questions.explanation: Besides income, people seek things like freedom, security, and respect, which are non-material goals.

questions.text: Total income of the country divided by its total population is called:
questions.explanation: Per capita income is also known as the 'average income' of a person in a country.

questions.text: Countries with per capita income of US$ 49300 per annum and above in 2019 are called:
questions.explanation: These are high-income countries, primarily located in Europe, North America, and parts of the Middle East.

questions.text: In which category does India fall based on its per capita income in 2019?
questions.explanation: India's per capita income in 2019 was US$ 6700 per annum (at purchasing power parity).

questions.text: What is the main limitation of using 'Average Income' as a measure of development?
options: It hides disparities in income distribution
questions.explanation: Average income does not show how income is actually distributed among the citizens.

questions.text: The number of children that die before the age of one year as a proportion of 1000 live births is:
questions.explanation: IMR is a crucial indicator of the quality of healthcare and nutrition in a region.

questions.text: Literacy rate measures the proportion of literate population in the age group of:
questions.explanation: In India, any person aged 7 or above who can read and write with understanding is considered literate.

questions.text: Which state in India has the lowest Infant Mortality Rate according to the textbook data?
questions.explanation: Kerala's low IMR is attributed to its strong focus on basic health and educational facilities.

questions.text: Public facilities like education and health are important because:
options: Money in your pocket cannot buy everything you need to live well
questions.explanation: Many essential services like a pollution-free environment or protection from infectious diseases cannot be bought individually.

questions.text: What is the Body Mass Index (BMI) of a person who is undernourished?
questions.explanation: BMI is calculated as weight divided by height squared (kg/m²). A value below 18.5 indicates undernourishment.

questions.text: The Human Development Report is published annually by:
questions.explanation: The United Nations Development Programme (UNDP) uses health, education, and income to rank countries.

questions.text: Life Expectancy at birth denotes:
options: Average expected length of life of a person
Total number of years a person lives
questions.explanation: It is an estimate of the number of years a newborn is expected to live based on current mortality rates.

questions.text: Sustainable development refers to development that meets the needs of the present without compromising:
options: The ability of future generations to meet their own needs
questions.explanation: Sustainability ensures that resources are used judiciously so they are available for the future.

questions.text: Which of the following is a renewable resource?
questions.explanation: Groundwater is replenished by nature (rain), though it can be overused.

questions.text: Consequences of environmental degradation do not respect ________ boundaries.
questions.explanation: Pollution and climate change are global issues that affect everyone regardless of where they live.

questions.text: Different persons can have different developmental goals and what may be development for one:
options: May not be development for the other
questions.explanation: For example, a dam may provide electricity to industrialists but displace local tribals.

questions.text: The proportion of children in the 14-15 age group attending school is called:
questions.explanation: This ratio indicates the level of participation in secondary education.

questions.text: A country's HDI rank is based on which of the following?
questions.explanation: The Human Development Index is a composite statistic of these three dimensions.

questions.text: Which neighboring country of India has a better performance in terms of human development than India (based on 2019 data)?
questions.explanation: Sri Lanka is much ahead of India in all parameters of the Human Development Index.

questions.text: Groundwater is an example of overuse in which regions of India?
questions.explanation: Agricultural intensity in these regions has led to a significant decline in groundwater levels.

questions.text: Non-renewable resources are those which will:
options: Be exhausted after years of use
questions.explanation: These resources have a fixed stock on earth and cannot be replenished.

questions.text: Development is a ________ process.
questions.explanation: The desire for improvement and the questions about how we ought to live are always with us.

questions.text: The PDS (Public Distribution System) helps in maintaining the ________ status of the people.
questions.explanation: By providing food grains at subsidized rates, it ensures basic nutrition for the poor.

questions.text: Per capita income is also called:
questions.explanation: It averages the national income over the entire population.

questions.text: Kerala's high literacy rate is a result of:
options: Strong government focus on primary education
questions.explanation: The state government prioritized social sectors like education and health for decades.

questions.text: What is the developmental goal for a landless rural labourer?
options: More days of work and better wages
questions.explanation: For someone without land, basic employment and survival are the primary goals.

questions.text: What does 'National Development' imply?
options: A country's ability to improve the well-being of its citizens
questions.explanation: It refers to the holistic improvement in the living standards and opportunities for all people in a nation.

questions.text: Which of these is NOT a component of HDI?
questions.explanation: While important, environmental factors are not currently direct components of the standard HDI formula.

questions.text: A prosperous farmer from Punjab would likely want:
options: Cheap labour and high family income
questions.explanation: Their goal is to maximize profit through low costs and high selling prices.

questions.text: Crude oil is a ________ resource.
questions.explanation: Once used, it cannot be replaced by natural processes in a human timeframe.

questions.text: Sustainability of development is a relatively ________ area of knowledge.
questions.explanation: Scientists, economists, and philosophers are only recently working together on this critical issue.

questions.text: High per capita income is a ________ condition for development, but not sufficient.
questions.explanation: While wealth is needed, health and education are equally important for true development.

questions.text: Which of the following is a material goal?
questions.explanation: Material goals are those that involve physical goods and services that can be bought.

questions.text: The World Development Report is published by:
questions.explanation: The World Bank uses its report to classify nations by their economic status.

questions.text: How many days of work does MGNREGA guarantee?
questions.explanation: The Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act ensures 100 days of manual work.

questions.text: The BMI of an adult is calculated by dividing weight in kg by:
questions.explanation: The formula is weight (kg) / [height (m)]².

questions.text: Development involves thinking about the ________ and the ways in which we can work towards our goals.
questions.explanation: Development is inherently forward-looking and aspirational.
